chunks.title: Catalog Description
chunks.content: Foundations of Software Engineering. (3-2). Credit 4. Intensive programming experience and provision of the fundamentals needed for larger-scale software development; integration of concepts in computer science and familiarization with a variety of programming and development tools and techniques; team projects each with an emphasis on a different specialization within computer science; emphasis on programming techniques to ease code integration and clarity; practical exposure to software-engineering processes through large-scale projects and specification and documentation. Prerequisite: Grade of C or better in CSCE 314, CSCE 350, or ECEN 350; grade of C or better or concurrent enrollment in CSCE 313.

chunks.title: Course Prerequisites
chunks.content:
Prerequisite/Corequisite(s): Grade of C or better in CSCE 314,
CSCE 350/ECEN 350, or ECEN 350/CSCE 350; grade of C or better or concurrent
enrollment in CSCE 313.

chunks.title: Course Learning Outcomes
chunks.content:
Upon completion of this course, the learner will be able to:
• Explain the need for software engineering through analysis of industry
examples and personal experience.
• Apply fundamental concepts of software construction, including
requirements management, design, implementation, testing, and
deployment.
• Design and develop software that is clear, maintainable, and integrates
current software technologies, including new and unfamiliar tools.
• Collaborate and communicate effectively in small teams to achieve project
goals.
• Produce technical documentation using characteristics of effective technical
writing.
• Analyze software system requirements for portability, performance, and
testability.
• Employ coding layout and style conventions to enhance code clarity and ease
of integration.
• Utilize software tools and APIs to augment software functionality and
maintainability.
• Implement database solutions and design user interfaces that meet project
specifications.

chunks.title: Grading Policy
chunks.content:
The grading will be based on various class activities:
1. Writing: 35% (Project 1: 300 + 300 [revision]; Project 2: midterm report 400 + final
report 500; Project 3: project ideation 250 + midterm report 400 + final 500). To
receive a passing grade in this course, you have to get a passing grade on the
writing component: weighted total 70/100 (equivalent to 'C" or better).
2. Three projects: 50% : 1 individual project (15%) and 2 team projects (project 2: 15%,
project 3: 20%),
Project 1 [Individual]: Web-site building and deployment. HTML, JavaScript, CSS,
PHP, regex, Docker
Project 2 [team]: Corporate database (personnel, sales, customer, branches,
inventory, etc.). Waterfall development paradigm. Java DB connectivity (JDBC). SQL
database. Java GUI API (Swing, etc.). AWS or Docker
Project 3 [team]: Agile development paradigm. Google apps scripts development (G-
suite add-on: Doc, Sheet, Slides, etc.). Apps Scripts (Google's cloud-based
JavaScript). HCl components.
3. Honors project: 7%: This is a semester-long individual side project, fully utilizing Al
tools. Similar in spirit with Google's "20% time". You get to choose what to do. You
are expected to set aside ~2 hours / week on this side project. Grading will be based
on final presentation. Weekly commits are required.
• Note that for all other projects, you are only allowed to use Al tools for dev
environment setup and other tasks explicitly stated in the project description.
4. Attendance: 4%, based on attendance. Each absence will deduct 1% (out of 4%) of
the attendance score (this includes both the lecture and the lab). Excused absences
will only be allowed for those allowed in the university rules. UPDATE: Up to 10
undocumented absences will be excused (no questions asked).
5. Quizzes: 4% (total of 2, 2% each)
There will be three projects in the course. Specific grading practices for each project will
be announced when that project is given out, but the grade may include factors such as
evaluation of code clarity, teamwork, etc. Peer evaluation may be used as a significant
contributing factor to these grades (see below).
The projects are scored by the team, however, different individual contribution can lead
to differential grades given the same team score.
• i = individual score
• t = team score
• c = your contribution (X%, e.g., 25%). Sum of c for all members in the team should
equal 100.
• d = contribution divisor (Y%, e.g., 25% for a team of 4, 33.3% for a team of 3, etc.)
• m = max score attainable = 110
• Formula: i = min(sqrt(c/d) * t, m).
• Examples:
1. Team got 90 and your contribution was 25% for a 4-person team.
= min (sqrt(25/25) * 90, 110) = min(1*90, 110) = 90.
2. Team got 85 and your contribution was 45% for a 4-person team (your team
mates slacked off).
i = min (sqrt(45/25) * 85, 110) = min(114.03, 110) = 110.
3. Team got 95 and your contribution was 20% for a 3-person team.
min (sqrt(20/33.333) * 95, 110) = min(73.587, 110) = 73.587
The grading scheme will be: 89.5: A; 79.5: В; 69.5: C; 59.5: D; all else F.

chunks.title: Late Work Policy
chunks.content:
Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not
considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy (Student Rule 7).

chunks.title: Course Specific Late Work Policy
chunks.content:
Late penalty: 1 point (out of 100) per hour. Late submissions will not be accepted 4 days
after the deadline and/or after the solution has been posted.

chunks.title: AI Statement
chunks.content:
You are not allowed to use any Al tools to solve your homework problems (both writing
and coding), unless explicitly allowed by the instructor.
